async_execution.mechanism: Hooks run synchronously; no async/background flag documented.

context_injection.mechanism: Hooks can inject context at session initialization via the sessionStart event, enabling custom context to be added to the agent's active session at startup.

decision_control.mechanism: Exit codes drive decisions: exit code 2 blocks the action, exit code 0 allows it to proceed; JSON on stdout can also return structured decision fields.

handler_types.mechanism: Two handler types documented: command (shell scripts communicating over stdio via JSON) and prompt (LLM-evaluated natural language conditions for policy enforcement without custom scripting).

hook_scopes.mechanism: Four scopes documented in priority order: Enterprise (system-wide MDM-managed), Team (cloud-distributed for Enterprise plans), Project (.cursor/hooks/ in the repository), and User (global personal configuration at ~/.cursor/hooks/).

input_modification.mechanism: Cursor hooks are not documented to rewrite tool input before execution.

json_io_protocol.mechanism: Command hooks receive event data as JSON on stdin and may return structured JSON on stdout to signal decisions and supply output fields.

matcher_patterns.mechanism: Hook entries accept a matcher string that filters which tool or event fires the hook (for example a specific shell command or tool name).

permission_control.mechanism: Cursor hooks control allow/block via exit codes but do not govern tool availability through permission rule updates.

providerExtensions.name: Cursor hook event set
providerExtensions.summary: Cursor documents lifecycle events for both Agent (sessionStart, preToolUse, beforeShellExecution, afterFileEdit, stop, and related pre/post tool hooks) and Cursor Tab inline completions (beforeTabFileRead and related Tab-specific hooks).

providerExtensions.name: Command (shell) handler type
providerExtensions.summary: Command-based hooks execute shell scripts that receive JSON via stdin; exit code 0 indicates success and exit code 2 blocks actions.

providerExtensions.name: Prompt (LLM) handler type
providerExtensions.summary: Prompt-based hooks leverage an LLM to evaluate natural language conditions, enabling policy enforcement without custom scripting — the model determines whether to allow or block based on a natural language rule.

providerExtensions.name: Millisecond timeout field
providerExtensions.summary: Hook entries accept a timeout in milliseconds that limits how long a command may run before being terminated.

providerExtensions.name: statusMessage output field
providerExtensions.summary: Hooks may surface a short camelCase statusMessage to the agent to explain a decision or provide feedback.

providerExtensions.name: Matcher group failClosed flag
providerExtensions.summary: Matcher groups may set failClosed to treat hook failures as blocking (alias for the blocking semantic) rather than non-fatal warnings.

providerExtensions.name: Matcher group loop_limit flag
providerExtensions.summary: Matcher groups may bound how many times the hook fires within a loop to guard against runaway tool sequences.

providerExtensions.name: Hooks wired through .cursor/settings.json
providerExtensions.summary: Hook files under .cursor/hooks/ are activated via entries in .cursor/settings.json; enabling or disabling a hook means editing the settings file, not moving the hook file itself.

providerExtensions.name: Enterprise and Team hook scopes
providerExtensions.summary: Cursor supports Enterprise (MDM-managed system-wide) and Team (cloud-distributed for Enterprise plans) hook scopes above the Project and User levels, enabling organization-wide hook policy deployment.

providerExtensions.name: Cursor Tab inline completion hooks
providerExtensions.summary: Separate hook events (e.g., beforeTabFileRead) fire for Cursor Tab inline completion operations, distinct from Agent hook events, enabling separate control policies for inline vs. Agent-mode completions.

providerExtensions.name: Partner ecosystem hooks
providerExtensions.summary: Cursor partners with ecosystem vendors (MintMCP, Semgrep, Snyk, 1Password, and others) who have built pre-built hook integrations covering security scanning, governance, and secrets management.
